14.reply:
You're not the one who made the mistakes, and this is fixable. Under-reported income discovered and voluntarily corrected is a routine thing tax offices deal with constantly. It is not fraud, it is not a crime when you're the one flagging it, and community sports clubs rarely owe much (or anything) once corrected. Breathe. You have time to do this properly.

Here's what actually matters, roughly in order.

**Stop the bleeding first.** Don't touch or "clean up" the old records. Don't delete anything, don't overwrite spreadsheets, don't rewrite entries to make them look tidy. You need the original mess preserved exactly as-is, because that's your evidence that the errors were genuine bookkeeping mistakes and not concealment. Make a backup copy of everything today and set it aside.

**Don't file or send anything to the tax office yet.** The instinct when panicking is to fire off a confession email tonight. Don't. A wrong or half-formed disclosure is harder to walk back than silence. Get the numbers straight first, then disclose in one clean go.

**This is a committee matter, not yours alone.** You're a volunteer who inherited someone else's error. Tell your club president or committee chair now, in writing, plainly: what you found, roughly how big it looks, and that you're getting it corrected. This does three things: it stops you carrying it alone, it makes the fix an official club decision rather than something you did unilaterally, and it protects you personally if anyone later asks why. Get the committee's authority (a minuted decision) to spend money on an accountant and to make a disclosure.

**Get an accountant or bookkeeper who knows not-for-profits.** This is the one thing worth paying for. You need someone to (a) work out the real income figures for both years, (b) tell you what, if anything, the club actually owes, and (c) handle the voluntary disclosure to the tax office. Voluntary disclosure almost always means reduced or waived penalties versus the tax office finding it themselves. That difference alone pays the accountant's fee. Depending on your club's turnover and where you are, there's a real chance the corrected position means little or no tax owed at all, sports clubs often have exemptions or low margins.

**Two things I can't answer without more from you**, and they change the specifics a lot: which country are you in (the tax office, the disclosure process, and whether the club is tax-exempt all differ), and roughly how large is the under-reporting, tens of dollars, or tens of thousands? A small figure may be a quick self-correction; a large one is squarely accountant territory.

On the panic itself: the worst outcome here isn't the error, it's mishandling the response, hiding it, guessing at numbers, or destroying records. You're doing the opposite by catching it and asking. Treasurers inherit predecessors' messes all the time. You found it, you're acting on it, and you're keeping the paper trail clean. That's exactly what a tax office wants to see from a volunteer, and it's why this ends up being paperwork rather than a disaster.

Tell me your country and a rough size, and I'll get more specific on the disclosure route and whether you likely owe anything.

15.reply:
You're in a stressful spot, but this is fixable, and it's genuinely more common than you'd think. Volunteer treasurers inherit messy books all the time. Nobody goes to jail over honest bookkeeping errors made by a predecessor and voluntarily corrected. Take a breath. Here's how to think about it.

**First, understand what you're actually dealing with.** You inherited errors. You didn't make them, and you're catching them, which is exactly what a responsible treasurer does. Tax offices distinguish sharply between honest mistakes that get corrected and deliberate concealment. The path you're on right now, discovering and fixing, is the good path. What turns a small problem into a real one is ignoring it once you know.

**Do not file anything or contact the tax office yet.** You don't have the numbers. Acting while panicking is how people make it worse. The sequence matters: figure out the scope first, then decide, then act.

A few things to do this week, roughly in order:

1. Don't touch or "clean up" the old records. Preserve everything the previous treasurer left, bank statements, spreadsheets, receipts, prior filings, exactly as they are. You may need to show the trail of what actually happened.

2. Reconstruct the real numbers for both years. Get the bank statements and work out actual income versus what was reported. You need to know the size of the gap before anyone can advise you properly. It might be smaller than your panic is telling you.

3. Loop in the committee, in writing. You're a volunteer officer, not the club. The management committee needs to know, and this protects you personally: you're the one who found it and raised it. A short factual note to the president or chair, "I've found discrepancies in the last two years of income reporting and I'm investigating the scope," is enough for now.

4. Get proper advice before you file anything. This is the one thing worth spending money on. A bookkeeper or accountant who handles small clubs and not-for-profits will know your jurisdiction's voluntary disclosure process, which almost every tax authority has, and which usually reduces or removes penalties when you come forward before they come to you. Many will do a first consult cheap or free. If the club can't afford it, a lot of jurisdictions have not-for-profit advisory services that help community clubs at no cost.

One thing I can't do well from here: give you the specific rules, because they depend entirely on where you are. The voluntary disclosure process, penalty relief, and reporting thresholds differ between, say, the ATO in Australia, HMRC in the UK, and the IRS in the US. Tell me your country (and state or region if it matters), roughly how big the club is, and rough numbers if you have them, and I'll get concrete about your actual disclosure route and what to expect.

What I'd steer you away from: don't quietly fix future filings and hope the past goes unnoticed, and don't let anyone on the committee talk you into that either. Correcting the past through the proper disclosure channel is what keeps this an administrative cleanup instead of something worse.

You caught it. That's the hard part done. Tell me where you're based and let's map the actual steps.

16.reply:
You're not the one who screwed up here, and this is fixable. Under-reported income corrected voluntarily is a routine thing tax offices deal with constantly. It is not fraud when you find an honest mistake and come forward. Breathe. Nobody is coming for you personally tonight.

Here's what to actually do, roughly in order.

Stop and preserve everything first. Don't "fix" the old records, don't delete anything, don't rewrite the previous treasurer's spreadsheets. Take copies of the bank statements, the books as they stand, and whatever the previous treasurer handed over. You want a clean before-picture so you can show exactly what was wrong and when. Adjusting records before you've documented the error is the one move that can make an innocent mistake look like a cover-up.

Don't report anything to the tax office yet. You're panicking, which means you don't yet know the real numbers. Reporting a wrong correction is its own headache. First you need to know how much, over which periods, and whether it even crosses a threshold that changes anything.

Get the committee in the loop. You're a volunteer, and this predates you. This is a club problem, not your private burden. Tell the president or chair now, in writing, plainly: what you found, that it spans about two years, that you're getting proper advice before acting. That written record protects you and it's the honest thing. A club decision like a voluntary disclosure needs the committee's backing, not one volunteer acting alone.

Get a professional to scope it before you do anything official. An accountant or bookkeeper who handles small clubs or not-for-profits can tell you in an hour or two whether this is a rounding-level nuisance or something bigger, and they'll know the right correction path. Most tax authorities have a voluntary disclosure route that substantially reduces or removes penalties when you come forward before they find it, which is exactly your situation. That process is the accountant's job to run, and getting the numbers right the first time matters more than speed.

A few things I can't answer without more from you. Which country and tax office is this? The rules, the disclosure programs, and the thresholds are completely different in Australia versus the UK versus the US. How big, roughly, do you think the gap is: hundreds, thousands, tens of thousands? And is the club incorporated or an unincorporated association? That changes who's actually liable. Tell me those and I can point you at the specific process.

One thing worth hearing clearly: honest bookkeeping errors, disclosed voluntarily, almost never land on the individual treasurer. Deliberate concealment is what gets people in trouble, and you're doing the opposite of that by asking this question. The worst realistic outcome is back-payment of tax owed plus some interest, paid by the club. That's a bill, not a catastrophe.

If you can tell me the country and rough size, I'll get specific about the next move.

17.reply:
You're going to be okay. This is fixable. Under-reporting income that a previous treasurer got wrong, discovered by you and disclosed voluntarily, is one of the most sympathetic situations a tax office deals with. Clubs sort this out all the time. Nobody sensible is coming after a volunteer who found someone else's mistake and put their hand up. Take a breath.

Here's what actually matters, in order.

Stop the bleeding first. Don't touch or "tidy up" the old records. Don't delete, overwrite, or correct anything in the previous years' books yet. You need the errors preserved exactly as they are, because the fix depends on showing what was reported versus what should have been. Right now your job is to understand the mess, not to clean it.

Get the numbers straight before you tell anyone anything. Pull the actual bank statements for the two years and reconcile them against what was reported. You're trying to answer one question: how much income went unreported, year by year? Until you have a real figure, you can't size the problem, and you'll scare yourself with worst-case guesses. It might be smaller than the panic suggests. Small clubs often sit under registration or reporting thresholds anyway, which can change the picture entirely.

Loop in the committee now, not later. You're the treasurer, but you are not personally on the hook for a predecessor's errors, and you should not be carrying this alone or making the disclosure call by yourself. Tell the president or chair today. This is a committee decision and a committee responsibility. Get it minuted that you discovered it and raised it immediately, that record protects you.

Get one hour with an accountant who knows your club's type. This is the piece I'd push hardest on. Before you contact the tax office, spend the money (the club's money) on a registered tax agent or accountant who handles community clubs or not-for-profits. They'll tell you three things you can't safely work out alone: whether the club owes anything at all given its structure and thresholds, exactly how a voluntary correction should be lodged, and whether there's a formal voluntary-disclosure route that reduces or removes penalties. Most tax systems treat a voluntary disclosure, one you make before they come knocking, far more leniently than something they catch. That difference is often the whole ballgame on penalties, so getting there first genuinely matters.

Then disclose, through the agent, on their advice. Voluntary correction, full explanation, the reconciled figures. Clubs do this and move on.

One thing I can't do from here is tell you the specific rules, thresholds, or disclosure process, because those depend entirely on where you are and what kind of entity the club is (incorporated association, its tax registrations, whether it's GST-registered, and so on). Tell me the country and roughly what the club is registered as, and I'll get more specific about the actual mechanism and what the thresholds look like.

What I'd do this week: reconcile the two years against the bank statements, tell the chair, book the accountant. That's the whole plan. The exposure is money plus possibly some penalty, and voluntary disclosure is designed to shrink the penalty. It is not a personal catastrophe, and it is not yours alone to fix.

What kind of entity is the club, and which country's tax office are we talking about?

18.reply:
I'm not going to touch the tools here. This is a decision that turns on facts about your club and your jurisdiction that I don't have, and the wrong move made confidently could make things worse. Let me be straight with you, which is what you asked for.

First, the thing you most need to hear: this is very likely fixable, and you are not in the trouble you feel like you're in right now. Under-reporting income caused by a predecessor's honest bookkeeping errors, discovered and voluntarily corrected by you, is close to the best-case version of a tax problem. Tax offices deal with this constantly. Voluntary disclosure by the person who found the mistake is treated very differently from evasion they catch on their own. The panic is real and understandable. The situation is mostly administrative.

Here's what actually matters, in order.

Do not file anything or contact the tax office yet. Not today. The instinct to "come clean immediately" is right in spirit but wrong in sequence, because a disclosure with wrong numbers or the wrong framing is harder to walk back than silence for a few more days. Get the facts first.

Stop the bleeding. Make sure the current year's books are being recorded correctly from now on, so you're not adding to the problem while you sort out the past.

Quantify it, roughly. You need to know the shape of this before anyone can advise you: how much income was under-reported, across which reporting periods, and whether it was one repeated error (say, a whole revenue stream left out) or scattered mistakes. Two years and "errors, plural" could mean a few hundred dollars or a lot more. The size changes who you need and how urgent this is. Pull the bank statements for the period and compare actual deposits against what was reported. You don't need it perfect, just the ballpark and the pattern.

Get a professional before you talk to the authorities. This is the one non-negotiable. An accountant or tax agent who handles small clubs and not-for-profits will know your jurisdiction's voluntary disclosure process, whether your club's income is even taxable (many small community sports clubs have exemptions or concessional treatment, and it's genuinely possible some or all of this income shouldn't have been taxed at all), and how to frame the disclosure so the club gets the reduced-penalty treatment that voluntary correction usually earns. This will cost some money and it is worth every cent. Do not DIY the disclosure to save a few hundred dollars.

Protect yourself and the committee. Two things here. Tell your committee or board now, in writing, plainly: what you found, that it predates you, and that you're getting professional advice before acting. This is partly the right thing and partly self-protection. You discovered and reported it; that record matters. Second, check whether the club has any officeholder liability insurance, and understand that in most cases a volunteer treasurer who inherits and reports a predecessor's mistakes is not personally on the hook. You didn't cause this and you're the one fixing it.

A few honest caveats. I don't know your country, and tax rules, disclosure programs, and the penalty relief you get for coming forward all vary by jurisdiction, so treat everything above as the general shape and let a local professional fill in the specifics. I also can't see your books, so I can't tell you whether this is small or serious. And go gently on the previous treasurer if you can, assuming it really was error and not something worse. Volunteer treasurers are usually well-meaning people doing a hard job without training.

Tell me what country the club is in and roughly how big the under-reporting looks once you've glanced at the statements, and I can get more specific about the actual disclosure process and whether the income was even taxable in the first place.

No blame language about the old treasurer, just the numbers.

3. Get an accountant or bookkeeper who knows not-for-profits. This is the step I'd push hardest on. A couple of hours of a professional's time is worth far more than my guidance here, because they'll know the exact voluntary-disclosure mechanism for your tax authority, whether the amounts even cross the threshold that requires amending, and how to file it so penalties are minimised or waived. Many jurisdictions actively reduce or drop penalties when you come forward before they find it. That's the whole game here: you disclose voluntarily rather than getting caught.

**The thing that will actually calm you down**

Two facts about how this really goes. Honest bookkeeping errors by volunteer treasurers are common and tax offices know it. The consequence for a genuine mistake, voluntarily corrected, is almost always: pay the tax that was owed, maybe some interest, and often little or no penalty. The severe outcomes you're imagining are for deliberate concealment, which is the opposite of what you're doing.

And you personally are almost certainly not liable for the money. In most club structures the association or its committee carries that, not the individual treasurer who inherited the mess. Don't let panic make you offer to pay it yourself or promise anything before you've talked to the committee and an accountant.

One thing not to do: don't call the tax office yourself, today, to confess before you have the numbers and advice. There's no deadline being missed by taking a week to prepare a proper voluntary disclosure, and going in unprepared can make it messier. Get your facts and your accountant first.

Tell me your country and rough size of the gap, and whether the club is incorporated, and I'll point you to the actual disclosure process and the specific forms.

20.reply:
You're not in the disaster you think you are, so take a breath. Under-reporting income by mistake, caught and corrected voluntarily, is a completely different animal from fraud. Tax offices deal with amended returns from small clubs constantly. The people who get hurt are the ones who hide it or ignore it, and you're doing the opposite by catching it. What matters now is that you handle it cleanly and don't make it worse in a panic.

Here's what to do, roughly in order.

Stop and preserve everything first. Don't "fix" or overwrite any of the old records, spreadsheets, or bank data. You need the original mess intact to reconstruct what actually happened, and later you may need to show your work. Make copies, work from the copies.

Reconstruct the real numbers before you tell anyone anything precise. Pull the bank statements for both years and rebuild actual income from them, line by line, against what was reported. You need to know the real figure, not a scary guess. The gap might be smaller than the panic in your head right now. Bank statements are the source of truth here, not the previous treasurer's books.

Tell the committee. You're the treasurer, but this isn't yours to carry alone, and it isn't yours to quietly fix alone either. The club's board or committee needs to know, in writing, minuted. This protects you: you found it, you reported it, the club decides how to proceed. If you patch it silently and it surfaces later, you own something you didn't create. Loop them in early.

Get one hour with an accountant or tax agent before you contact the tax office. This is the part worth spending a little money on, even for a small club. Someone who knows how your jurisdiction handles voluntary disclosures will tell you the exact mechanism, whether penalties get reduced for coming forward (they usually do, often substantially), and how to frame the correction. An hour of professional time now saves you from saying the wrong thing to the tax office. Most jurisdictions have a formal "voluntary disclosure" path that specifically rewards self-reporting with lower or waived penalties.

A few things that will calm you down if they're true. Many small sports clubs are wholly or partly tax-exempt or fall under mutuality rules, meaning member subscriptions may not even be assessable income. So "under-reported income" might not translate to "owed tax" at anything like the number you're imagining. The accountant will sort this out fast. And honest bookkeeping errors, disclosed voluntarily, almost never lead to anything criminal. That risk lives in concealment, not in mistakes.

Two things to be careful about. Don't sign or lodge anything with numbers you haven't personally reconstructed from the bank records. And don't publicly blame the former treasurer or put accusations in writing before you actually understand what happened. "Errors were made and are being corrected" is the tone. It may have been sloppiness, not anything worse, and you don't yet know which.

I don't know your country, and it matters, because the disclosure process and the exemption rules are jurisdiction-specific. Tell me where the club is and roughly what kind of income we're talking about (member fees, canteen takings, grants, sponsorship) and I can get more concrete about the exemption question and the exact disclosure route.

One reassurance to end on. The previous treasurer created this. You found it. Handled the way I've laid out, this becomes a tidy correction and a story about you being the person who caught it, not a scandal.

21.reply:
First, the human part: you are not the villain here. You inherited someone else's mistake, you found it, and you are trying to do the right thing. That instinct is exactly what gets clubs through this. Breathe. Nothing about this needs to be solved tonight, and panicking treasurers make bad calls, so let's slow it down.

I can't give you jurisdiction-specific legal advice, and I don't know which country's tax office you're dealing with, so treat everything below as a sensible sequence, not as compliance certainty. Where it matters, a name matters more than my general knowledge.

Here's what actually protects you and the club.

**Stop the bleeding, then leave the past alone for a moment.** Make sure the current year's books are being recorded correctly from today. Do not go back and "fix" or overwrite the old records yet. You need the original erroneous records preserved exactly as they are, because voluntary disclosures rest on showing what was reported versus what should have been. Rewriting them first destroys your own evidence and can look like concealment. Copy everything, work on copies.

**Get the numbers before you get scared of them.** Right now you have "two years of errors," which is a feeling, not a figure. Pull the bank statements and reconstruct actual income year by year. The gap might be smaller than the panic suggests, and it might be below thresholds that change how the tax office treats it. You cannot decide anything, or brief anyone, until you know the real number.

**Understand what kind of entity you are, because it may change everything.** Many community sports clubs are not-for-profit or fall under mutuality principles, where member subscriptions and internal income aren't taxable the way a business's revenue is. It's genuinely possible that some of the "under-reported income" was never taxable in the first place. Don't assume the worst-case tax bill is real until someone who knows your club's tax status confirms it.

**This is a club decision, not yours to carry alone.** You are a volunteer who found a problem. Take it to the committee or the board, in writing, promptly. Two reasons: the club, not you personally, is the taxpayer, and putting it on the record that you disclosed it the moment you found it is your own protection. A treasurer who reports upward looks nothing like one who sat on it.

**Then get a professional before you contact the tax office.** An accountant or tax agent who handles not-for-profits will tell you whether there's even a liability, how to quantify it, and how to make a voluntary disclosure. This matters enormously: in most tax systems, a voluntary disclosure made before the authority comes asking gets dramatically reduced penalties, sometimes with interest only and no penalty at all. Getting caught is a different world from coming forward. The professional is how you land on the right side of that line, and their fee is trivial against the penalty difference.

One thing to decide quickly, so you don't trip yourself: don't call or email the tax office yourself in a panic before you have the numbers and advice. A disorganised half-disclosure can lock you into wrong figures. Get the figures, get the advice, then disclose deliberately.

The honest reassurance: honest bookkeeping errors at a small volunteer-run club, self-reported, are about the most sympathetic case a tax office sees. This is a category they deal with constantly and have routine processes for. What turns it bad is hiding it or destroying records, and you're doing the opposite.

Tell me your country (and roughly what the club is: incorporated, member-based, its rough annual turnover) and I can get much more specific about your tax office's actual voluntary disclosure process and whether your income was even taxable.

22.reply:
First: breathe. You are not in trouble here. You inherited someone else's mistake and you're moving to fix it the moment you found it. That is the opposite of what tax offices penalise. Honest, voluntary correction by a volunteer who wasn't even there when the errors happened is the best possible position to be in.

Here's the straight version of what's going on and what to do.

**What this actually is.** Innocent under-reporting from bookkeeping errors is not fraud. Tax offices everywhere distinguish sharply between deliberate evasion and honest mistakes, and they treat voluntary disclosures far more leniently than errors they catch themselves. In most jurisdictions a voluntary correction before any audit or inquiry means reduced penalties or none, and the main thing you'll owe is the actual tax plus interest. You personally, as a volunteer who inherited this, are not on the hook the way you fear.

**Do these things, roughly in order.**

Stop and preserve. Don't delete, "clean up," or overwrite anything in the old records. Wrong figures and all, they're your evidence that this was error, not concealment. Freeze them as they are.

Tell the committee before you tell anyone else. This is a club decision, not yours to carry alone. The president and committee need to know today or this week, in writing, so it's on the record that you found it and raised it. You are the person who caught the problem. Make sure the minutes say so.

Get the numbers pinned down before you contact the tax office. You need to know how wrong things were: which years, how much income went unreported, roughly what tax that translates to. If it's small and simple, you may sort it yourself. If it spans two years and you're not certain of the figures, this is where you bring in an accountant or registered tax agent, ideally one who has handled a voluntary disclosure before. The club pays for this; it's a legitimate expense and money well spent.

Make a voluntary disclosure. Once you know the numbers, the club (usually through that accountant) approaches the tax office and corrects the record before they come asking. Doing it in this order, disclosure before any contact from them, is what unlocks the lenient treatment. If you've already heard from them, tell the accountant that immediately, because it changes the approach.

Fix the process so it can't recur. New reconciliation habits, a second set of eyes on the books, maybe proper software. This matters both for the club and because it shows the tax office you've closed the gap.

**Two things I don't know that change the specifics:** which country you're in, and whether the club is incorporated or a registered charity. Those determine which tax office, what the disclosure process is called, and whether there are separate reporting duties to a charities regulator. Tell me both and I'll get concrete.

**One honest caveat:** I'm not an accountant or a lawyer, and two years of under-reported income across an organisation is squarely in "get a professional to sign off" territory. The steps above are how to steer it; a tax agent is who should drive once the numbers are on the table. That's not me dodging, it's the genuinely right move given what's at stake.

You found this. You're acting on it. Tell me the country and the club's legal status and I'll make the next steps specific.

23.reply:
Take a breath. This is fixable, and you are not in the kind of trouble your gut is telling you you're in right now.

Here's the thing that should lower your heart rate: tax offices deal with under-reported income from small clubs and volunteer treasurers constantly. It's routine. What determines how this goes is not the error itself, it's whether you come to them or they come to you. You've just discovered it and you're already trying to fix it. That's the good side of the line.

And this matters legally and practically: honest bookkeeping mistakes by a predecessor are treated very differently from concealment. You didn't make the errors. You found them. Nobody is going to jail over a sports club's accounts, and personal liability for a volunteer acting in good faith is a very different animal from fraud. So put the worst-case movie away.

What to actually do, roughly in order:

**Stop and preserve, don't fix yet.** Don't start editing old records or "correcting" figures on your own. Pull together what you have, bank statements, the old books, tax filings for both years, and set the discovered errors aside as-is. You want a clean before-and-after, not a muddle.

**Work out the real number.** How much income was under-reported, across which periods? You may not be able to do this alone, and you shouldn't have to. Which brings me to the one thing I'd push you on.

**Get an accountant or bookkeeper who handles this, before you contact the tax office.** Not to hide anything, the opposite. A pro will size the actual liability, tell you the right disclosure process for your country, and often the correction plus a voluntary disclosure carries much smaller penalties than getting caught later. Many places actively reduce or waive penalties when you come forward first. This is the single highest-value move and it's worth the fee. If the club genuinely can't afford one, say so and I'll help you find low-cost or volunteer options, some sports associations and charity regulators have free advice lines.

**Tell your committee or board, in writing, now.** You're the treasurer, but this is the club's problem, not yours to carry alone or to quietly fix. A short factual note to the chair and committee: what you found, which years, that you're getting professional advice, what the next step is. This protects you and it's the right thing. Don't accuse the former treasurer of anything, just state what the records show.

**Then make the disclosure, with the accountant's help.** Corrected figures, the two years, and payment or a payment arrangement for what's owed. Do this proactively.

Two things I don't know that change the specifics: which country you're in, and roughly how much income we're talking about, ballpark. Tell me those and I can get concrete about the actual process, which regulator, and what penalty relief you're likely looking at.

One more time, because you're panicking and it's true: you did the hard part already, which is not looking away. The rest is procedure.

24.reply:
You're in a bad spot, but it's a fixable one, and the most important thing I can tell you is this: you didn't cause it, and honest disclosure of an inherited error is treated very differently from hiding one. Take a breath. Nobody is coming to arrest a volunteer sports club treasurer over bookkeeping mistakes he didn't make and is now trying to fix.

Here's the straight version of what's going on and what to do.

**Where you actually stand**

The tax office (I'm assuming ATO, given your setup, but this holds broadly) deals with under-reported income constantly. There's a whole standard process for it: you correct the prior periods, you pay what's owed, and where there's a shortfall you may owe interest and possibly a penalty. The penalty regime scales with culpability. An honest mistake that you voluntarily disclose sits at the bottom of that scale, and voluntary disclosure before they come looking usually reduces the penalty substantially, sometimes to nil. The thing that turns a manageable correction into a real problem is concealment, not the original error. So the danger now isn't the past two years. It's what you do in the next few weeks.

One caveat before you act on any of this: I don't know your club's structure (incorporated association, company limited by guarantee, unincorporated), your revenue size, or which specific taxes are affected (income tax, GST, PAYG if you have paid staff). Those change the mechanics. Treat what follows as the shape of the right path, not the filing itself.

**What to do, roughly in order**

Stop and preserve, don't fix-and-file yet. Do not go in and start editing old records or re-lodging anything on your own tonight. Right now your job is to understand the size of the hole, not to plug it single-handedly. Pull together the bank statements, the lodged returns, and whatever the previous treasurer left, and get a clear picture of how much income was missed and across which periods.

Tell your committee. This is the part people avoid and shouldn't. You are one office-holder of a club with a management committee, and this is a club liability, not your personal one. The committee needs to know, it should be minuted, and the decision to disclose should be the club's decision, made on the record. That record also protects you personally: it shows you found the problem and raised it rather than sitting on it.

Get a registered tax agent or accountant involved before you contact the tax office. This is the one step I'd push hardest on. An agent who does small not-for-profits will know the exact disclosure route, will quantify the shortfall properly, and can make the voluntary disclosure on the club's behalf in the way that minimises penalties. For a two-year correction across a club's books, the cost of an hour or two of their time is small against getting the disclosure framed correctly. If money is tight, many accountants will do a first consult cheaply, and some peak bodies for community sport offer free or subsidised governance help.

Then make the voluntary disclosure. With the agent, you correct the affected periods and lodge the disclosure. You'll likely owe the tax that should have been paid plus interest; the penalty is where voluntary disclosure earns its keep. Ask the agent specifically about a payment arrangement, because a club can usually pay a shortfall in instalments rather than in one hit, which matters if the club doesn't have the cash sitting there.

**A few things worth knowing**

Don't try to figure out or assign blame to the old treasurer right now. If it was genuine error, that's the whole story and it stays a governance footnote. Keep your focus on the disclosure. Any question of whether it was more than error is a separate conversation the committee can have later, and probably one for the agent to flag if they see signs of it.

Check whether your club carries office-holders' or association liability insurance. Many incorporated associations do. It's worth knowing what's there, though for an inherited honest bookkeeping error your personal exposure as the current treasurer is low regardless.

Keep a dated file of everything from here on: when you found it, what you found, when you told the committee, when you engaged the agent. That paper trail is your best friend if anyone ever asks whether the club acted in good faith.

The realistic outcome, if you do this properly, is: back-tax paid, interest paid, penalty small or waived, matter closed, and a note in the minutes that the club fixed its own books. That's a bad month, not a catastrophe.

I'm not an accountant or a lawyer, and the exact figures and penalty treatment depend on details I don't have, so the registered agent is the person who turns this plan into the actual filing. But the first two moves you can make this week without spending a dollar: tell your committee and get it minuted, and book a call with a not-for-profit accountant. Do those two and you've already converted this from a secret you're carrying into a problem the club is handling.

25.reply:
You asked me to be straight, so here it is: this is a fixable problem, and the fact that you found it is good news, not bad. Underreported income by a volunteer club is common, tax offices deal with it constantly, and the standard path for someone who comes forward voluntarily is far gentler than for someone who gets caught. You are not in trouble the way your panic is telling you.

What matters now is doing this in the right order.

**Don't touch or "fix" anything yet.** Don't amend records, don't file anything, don't email the tax office. First you need to know the actual size of the problem, and you need the club's committee behind you before you act on its behalf. Acting alone as an individual on a club's tax position is how a volunteer ends up personally exposed. This is a club decision.

**Notify your committee / management chair now.** A short factual note: you've found bookkeeping errors spanning roughly two years that appear to have understated reported income, you're still quantifying it, and you want authority to engage an accountant. You are the person who found it and flagged it. Make sure that's on record in writing, dated today. That single fact protects you more than anything else.

**Then get a professional.** An accountant or bookkeeper who handles small clubs and not-for-profits. This is the real move. Most tax systems have a voluntary disclosure route that sharply reduces or eliminates penalties when you come forward before they come to you, but the mechanics, the time limits, and how far back you must go depend entirely on where you are and what kind of entity the club is. An accountant will scope the actual shortfall, tell you which years are still open, and handle the disclosure wording so it lands as cooperative rather than evasive. A few hundred dollars here can save thousands.

**Preserve everything, change nothing.** Bank statements, the old ledgers, spreadsheets, receipts, whatever the previous treasurer left. Make copies. Don't edit the historical records to make them "correct" now. Investigators and accountants both need to see what was actually recorded versus what should have been. A clean audit trail of the errors is an asset.

A few things worth knowing while the adrenaline settles. Honest bookkeeping errors are treated completely differently from deliberate fraud. Nothing you've described sounds deliberate, and you're the one raising your hand. The previous treasurer's mistakes are not your fault, and a treasurer who inherits a mess and reports it is behaving exactly as a treasurer should.

To give you sharper next steps I need three things: which country (and if Australia, that changes the specifics — GST, ACNC, ATO voluntary disclosure all come into play), whether the club is an incorporated association or charity or just an informal group, and roughly how big the underreported amount looks so far (hundreds, thousands, tens of thousands). Tell me those and I'll walk you through the specific disclosure route.

For tonight: message your chair, back up the files, breathe. Nothing here needs to be solved before morning, and coming forward is the thing that protects both you and the club.

26.reply:
You need to know this before anything else: the single most important thing is to fix this properly and voluntarily, and clubs in exactly your position deal with it and come out fine all the time. Under-reporting that you find and correct yourself is a categorically different situation from under-reporting the tax office catches. You are on the good side of that line right now. Stay on it.

Here is what actually matters, in order.

Do not touch the records. Do not "fix" or delete or overwrite anything in the old books to make them look right. Leave the errors exactly as they are and correct them going forward through proper amendments. Altering historical records is the one move that turns an honest mistake into something that looks like a cover-up, and it's completely unnecessary.

Pull together what you have. You need the bank statements, whatever bookkeeping records exist, and the returns that were actually filed for both years. You cannot know the size of the problem until you can compare income received against income reported. Right now you're panicking about an unknown number. Turn it into a known number first. It may be far smaller than it feels at 11pm.

Get a professional involved before you contact the tax office. An accountant or tax agent who works with community clubs and not-for-profits will tell you three things you can't work out alone: whether your club even owes tax on this income (many small sports clubs have exemptions, mutuality rules, or thresholds that change the whole picture), how to file the amendments correctly, and how to frame the voluntary disclosure so you get the reduced-penalty treatment that most tax offices offer for errors you report yourself. That last point is worth real money. Voluntary disclosure almost always means substantially lower penalties, and sometimes none.

Tell your committee, in writing, now. You are the treasurer, but this is the club's liability, not yours personally, and the decision to spend money on an accountant and approach the tax office belongs to the committee or board. Send a short, factual note: what you found, roughly which periods, that you've touched nothing, and that you're recommending professional advice before contacting the tax office. This does two things. It protects you (you surfaced it the moment you found it), and it makes the response the club's decision rather than yours to carry alone.

A few things that should lower your heart rate. Your predecessor's errors are not your fault, and correcting them is exactly what a good treasurer does. Tax offices see self-reported bookkeeping errors from volunteer-run clubs constantly, and their default posture toward voluntary correction is to work with you, not to punish you. Interest on unpaid amounts is normal and expected; large penalties for honest, self-reported error usually are not. Nobody is going to show up at the clubhouse.

One thing I don't know and you shouldn't guess at: which country and tax authority you're under, because the specific programs, deadlines, and thresholds differ. Tell me that and I can get more concrete about the disclosure process and whether your club is even likely to owe.

The move for tonight is small: write the note to your committee and stop looking at the numbers until an accountant is looking at them with you. Everything else can wait for daylight.

27.reply:
You're not in as much trouble as your gut is telling you right now. Under-reporting income to the ATO by a small volunteer club, caused by a predecessor's honest mistakes, that you found and are moving to fix, is close to the best possible version of a tax problem. Voluntary disclosures of this kind are routine, and the ATO treats "we found it and came to you" very differently from "we caught you." Breathe. Nothing here needs to be solved tonight.

A few things before you do anything.

Don't touch or "clean up" the old records. No deleting, no back-editing entries to make them look right, nothing. The original messy books are your evidence that these were genuine errors, not concealment, and that distinction is what keeps this in the small-penalty lane. Leave them exactly as they are.

Now the honest limits of what I can tell you. I don't know which country you're in (I've assumed Australia from context, and I might be wrong), what the club's legal structure is (incorporated association? company limited by guarantee?), whether it's GST-registered, or what "income" means here. That last one matters more than it sounds: a lot of small sports clubs are income-tax-exempt as not-for-profits, and much of what a club collects (member subs, fundraising among members) can fall under mutuality principles and not be assessable income at all. It's genuinely possible the "under-reported income" isn't taxable in the first place, and the real fix is amended lodgements with no tax owing. Don't assume the worst-case number is the real number.

What actually needs to happen, in order:

1. Get the raw picture. Pull together what was reported versus what should have been for each affected year. You don't need it perfect, just the rough size of the gap. That number tells you and any advisor how serious this really is.

2. Tell your committee. You're the treasurer, but you're not the person who should carry this alone or decide it alone. This is a governance matter for the whole committee/board. Put it in writing to them, plainly, and minute it. That paper trail showing you raised it the moment you found it is worth a lot.

3. Get one hour with an accountant who knows not-for-profits. This is the step that actually resolves it, and it's the one I can't substitute for. They'll tell you whether the income was even assessable, whether you amend prior returns or use the ATO's voluntary disclosure process, and roughly what (if anything) is owed. An hour of a registered tax agent's time is cheap against the anxiety you're carrying, and the club should pay for it, not you.

On money and penalties, so the fear has a shape: where tax genuinely is owed, you'd expect back-tax plus interest, and possibly a penalty. The penalty for an unprompted voluntary disclosure of an inadvertent error is heavily reduced, often close to nil, precisely because you came forward. This is not the territory of fraud investigations or personal liability for you. You inherited this and you're fixing it.

One thing to protect yourself: whatever you do, do it through the committee and in writing, and keep your own dated notes of when you found this and what you did next. Not because anyone's coming for you, but because a clean record of a treasurer acting properly the moment they found a problem is exactly what makes this a non-event.

If you tell me the country, the club's legal structure, and roughly what the club takes in and from where (member fees, canteen, grants, sponsorship), I can get much more specific about whether this is even taxable and which ATO process fits. But the first move tonight is nothing more than: don't alter the old books, and write a short note to your committee. The rest waits for daylight and an accountant.

28.reply:
First — breathe. This is fixable. Under-reporting income caused by a predecessor's honest bookkeeping mistakes is one of the most common things tax offices deal with, and voluntary disclosures like the one you're about to make are treated very differently from evasion they catch on their own. You're not in trouble for finding this. You'd only be in trouble if you buried it.

Here's the straight version of what to do.

**Do not touch the old records.** Don't "clean them up," don't correct entries, don't delete anything. Whatever the previous treasurer produced is now evidence of an honest error, and it needs to stay exactly as it is. Make a backup copy of the current state today (photos, PDFs, a copy of the accounting file) and set it aside.

**Get a number, roughly.** Before you talk to anyone, you need a ballpark of how much income went unreported and over which periods. You don't need it perfect. You need to know whether we're talking about a few hundred dollars or tens of thousands, because that changes who you loop in and how urgent this is.

**Tell the committee, in writing, now.** You're the treasurer, but you're not the decision-maker on something this size — the club's committee or board is. Send them a short factual note: what you found, roughly how big, that no money is missing (assuming that's true — check), and that you're recommending the club get professional advice and make a voluntary disclosure. This protects you personally. The person who found the problem and raised it immediately is never the one who carries the blame.

**Get an accountant before you contact the tax office.** This is the one step I'd push hardest on. A registered accountant or tax agent who handles voluntary disclosures will know the exact mechanism in your country, will often deal with the tax office on the club's behalf, and will make sure the disclosure is framed correctly. Many tax authorities reduce or waive penalties heavily when you come forward voluntarily and the cause was error rather than deception. That reduction is worth far more than the fee. If the club has an existing accountant or auditor, call them first.

**Check whether money is actually missing.** Bookkeeping errors and theft look different. Under-reported income usually means the numbers were recorded wrong, not that cash walked. But confirm it: does the bank balance reconcile to what the club should have? If there's a genuine shortfall you can't explain, that's a separate and more serious conversation, and you'd want the committee's chair involved immediately.

A few things I'm not certain about, because I don't know where you are or how the club is structured. Which tax office (income tax, a sales/goods tax like GST or VAT, or payroll) and which country matters a lot for the disclosure process and the penalty rules. And whether the club is incorporated, a charity, or an unincorporated association changes who's legally responsible. Tell me the country and the club's legal form and I can get more specific.

One reassurance to hold onto while you sort this out: volunteer treasurers are not expected to be forensic accountants, and tax offices know it. You inherited someone else's mistake and you're fixing it the moment you saw it. That's the right thing, and it's the thing that keeps this small.

What country is the club in, and do you know roughly how large the gap is?

29.reply:
First and highest priority: what you're describing is an honest mistake by someone else that you discovered and want to fix. That is the best possible position to be in with a tax office. Voluntary disclosure of a prior treasurer's errors, before any audit, is treated very differently from concealment. You are not in trouble. Breathe.

Here's what to actually do, roughly in order.

Stop and preserve everything first. Don't touch or "clean up" the old records. Don't delete emails, rename files, or fix entries in the books yet. Make a copy of the bank statements, the accounting file or spreadsheets, and whatever the previous treasurer left you, exactly as they are. If it later needs correcting, you want the original state provable.

Then figure out the actual size of the problem before you decide anything. "Under-reporting income for two years" could mean a few hundred dollars of membership fees that slipped through, or something much larger. Pull the bank statements for the whole period and compare total money-in against what was actually declared. You want one number: how much income went unreported, per reporting period. Until you have that, the panic is running ahead of the facts, and the fix could turn out to be small.

Get a bookkeeper or accountant involved, ideally one who has handled a not-for-profit or small club. This is the single most useful thing you can do, and for a community club it's usually a few hundred dollars, not a fortune. They'll tell you whether this even crosses a threshold that matters, whether the club's tax status changes anything (many small sporting clubs are treated as not-for-profit or mutual organisations and a chunk of member income may not be taxable in the first place, which can shrink the "problem" dramatically), and they'll handle the actual amended filings. Do not file corrections yourself off a panic reading of the numbers.

Voluntary disclosure is your friend, and the accountant will run it. Most tax offices have a formal voluntary-disclosure process, and coming forward before they come to you almost always reduces or removes penalties and shows good faith. The accountant knows the mechanics and the wording. The order matters: understand the number, then disclose, not the reverse.

Loop in your committee, in writing. You're the treasurer, but this is a club liability, not your personal one, and the committee needs to know and to authorise spending money on an accountant. Send a short factual note: what you found, what period it covers, that you're getting professional advice, that you intend to disclose voluntarily. This does two things. It gets you a decision-making body behind you, and it puts on record that you acted the moment you found it, which protects you personally.

Two things to be careful about. One, resist the urge to quietly fix it and move on. Discovered-and-disclosed is a strong position; discovered-and-buried is the one that turns an error into a real problem. Two, keep your own conduct clean and documented from today. Dated notes of what you found and when, every step you take. If anyone ever asks, your file answers for you.

One thing I can't tell you: I don't know your country, so I can't name the specific tax office, the disclosure programme, the club's exact tax treatment, or the thresholds. All of that is real and knowable, and it's exactly what the accountant is for. If you tell me where the club is, I can get more specific about the process and what to expect.

The likely outcome, honestly: you get the number, an accountant files amended returns and a voluntary disclosure, the club pays the tax it genuinely owed plus possibly modest interest, and penalties are reduced or waived because you came forward. It costs some money and some awkward committee conversations. It is very unlikely to be the disaster it feels like right now.

30.reply:
You're going to be okay, and this is fixable. Under-reporting caused by a predecessor's bookkeeping errors is one of the most common things tax offices deal with, and voluntary correction is a well-worn path with predictable, survivable outcomes. Take a breath. You inherited this; you didn't cause it.

Here's the honest picture and what to actually do.

**What this is (and isn't).** Innocent errors by a prior treasurer are not fraud. Tax offices distinguish sharply between people who made mistakes and came forward, and people who hid things. The moment you voluntarily disclose, you move firmly into the first camp, and penalties for voluntary disclosure are typically reduced heavily or waived. The main cost is usually the tax that was genuinely owed, plus some interest. That's a cash problem, not a catastrophe.

**Do not do anything hasty tonight.** Don't call the tax office yet, don't fire off an email admitting things, don't touch or "clean up" the old records. Panic disclosures made before you understand the numbers tend to be wrong, and a wrong disclosure creates its own mess. You need the facts first.

The order I'd work in:

1. **Freeze and preserve the records.** Whatever the old treasurer left, bank statements, receipts, spreadsheets, keep it exactly as-is. Don't correct or delete anything. You may need to show the trail later, and altered records look far worse than messy ones.

2. **Tell the committee, in writing, this week.** You are one volunteer on a committee, and this is not yours to carry alone or to fix unilaterally. The club's management committee needs to know, and the decision to disclose and to spend money on advice is theirs to make, not yours. Put it in an email or bring it to the next meeting minutes: what you found, that you're getting professional advice, no blame language. This also protects you personally, you're the one who surfaced it.

3. **Get a professional to scope the actual numbers before you report anything.** This is the step that matters most. An accountant or tax agent, ideally one who has handled voluntary disclosures for small clubs or not-for-profits, will tell you how far back it goes, how much was actually under-reported, whether the club even owes tax (many small sports clubs have concessions, exemptions, or mutuality rules that mean far less is owing than you fear), and exactly how to make the disclosure so it counts as voluntary. A few hundred dollars of advice here can save the club thousands and will stop you from over-disclosing out of fear. The club pays for this, not you.

4. **Then disclose, through the accountant, on their advice.** Voluntary correction almost always beats waiting to be found. But you disclose once, correctly, with the numbers scoped, not in a panic.

A few things worth knowing now, so the fear doesn't fill the gaps. You almost certainly have personal protection here: committee members of incorporated associations acting honestly and in good faith are generally shielded from personal liability, and you inheriting and reporting an error is about as good-faith as it gets. The debt, if there is one, is the club's, not yours. And two years of small-club under-reporting is a modest matter to a tax office, not a headline.

One thing I can't give you precisely: the exact penalty regime, disclosure form, and liability rules depend on your country and how your club is legally structured (incorporated association, unincorporated, charity, etc.). Tell me where the club is and roughly its type and size, and I'll walk you through the specific process and what the accountant is likely to say. If you want, I can also draft the plain, no-blame note to your committee so that part's off your plate tonight.

You found a problem the last person hid or missed, and you're dealing with it properly. That's the treasurer doing the job right.
